evals.prompt: Should I run a 20% off sitewide promo next week to hit my quarterly number?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the discount-as-acquisition-tax versus anchor-erosion framework before answering. Pulls margin by product to compute the contribution-margin cost. Asks who the marginal customer is and whether a sitewide discount is the right tool versus a targeted one. Should not give a yes/no without diagnosis.
assertions: Walks through the acquisition-tax vs anchor-erosion frame
Calls modern.sales.margin_by_product to compute the cost
Asks who the marginal customer is
Recommends targeted over sitewide when marginal customer is a small share
Computes the contribution-margin math explicitly
Does not give a yes/no without diagnosis
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: We promote 16 times a year. Is that too often?
evals.expected_output: Identifies 16 promos per year (roughly every 3 weeks) as discount-trained. Pulls AOV and conversion rate by week to show the pre-promo dead-zone effect. Recommends lengthening the cycle over 6-12 months. Names the reset cost and warns that customer file relearning takes time.
assertions: Identifies 16x per year as discount-trained
Calls modern.sales.aov grouped by promo_status to show the gap
Names the pre-promo dead-zone effect on full-price weeks
Recommends lengthening cycle to ~4-6 promos per year
Acknowledges the 6-12 month reset cost
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our first-time customer discount is 15% off. Is that the right number?
evals.expected_output: Pulls cohort LTV by acquisition-discount band before answering. Frames the first-time discount as acquisition tax and asks whether LTV math supports it. Compares 15% against typical 10-15% one-time cap and discusses why subscription first-month discounts can run heavier than one-time discounts.
assertions: Calls modern.retention.cohort_ltv grouped by acquisition_discount_band
Frames first-time discount as acquisition tax
Cites typical 10-15% one-time discount band
Distinguishes one-time first-order from subscription first-month asymmetry
Recommends comparing LTV of full-price acquired vs discount-acquired cohorts
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Black Friday is coming. How deep should we discount and for how long?
evals.expected_output: Applies the BFCM and event-pricing discipline framework. Recommends a tiered structure (10-15% sitewide, 25% specific categories, 35% clearance) over flat sitewide. Discusses duration trade, suppression of full-price launches inside the window, pre-announcement, and post-promo restoration period.
assertions: Recommends tiered structure over flat sitewide
Cites specific tier depths (10-15 / 25 / 35)
Discusses duration trade between one-day and two-week BFCM
Recommends suppressing full-price launches inside the window
Recommends pre-announcement over surprise
Names the post-promo restoration period
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our subscription has 20% off first month. Should we make it 20% off forever to compete?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the subscription discount asymmetry. 20% off first month is recoverable on subsequent months at full price; 20% off forever is a permanent LTV compression and a different decision entirely. Walks through the cost math of each and warns against the conversion.
assertions: Identifies the asymmetry between first-month and ongoing subscription discount
Warns against the conversion to off-forever
Names off-forever as a permanent margin floor decision
Walks through the cost math of each
Recommends harder first-month over off-forever if competition requires response
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Customers keep saying our products are too expensive. Should I lower prices?
evals.expected_output: Asks whether the hesitation is actually price-elasticity or something else (trust, uncertainty, fit). Walks through the no-discount-but-value-add alternatives. Pulls margin and channel data to evaluate. Recommends testing value-add alternatives before a structural price cut. Distinguishes a permanent price cut from a discount.
assertions: Asks whether hesitation is actually price-elasticity
Walks through value-add alternatives (free shipping threshold, GWP, extended returns, loyalty credit)
Distinguishes a permanent price cut from a discount
Recommends testing value-add before structural price change
Calls modern.sales.margin_by_product to evaluate room
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our 15% discount on a $30 product with 30% margin. What's the actual cost?
evals.expected_output: Computes the contribution-margin math explicitly. 15% off $30 = $25.50 revenue. Landed cost 70% of $30 = $21. New contribution margin $4.50 on $25.50 vs $9 on $30. Margin retained is 50%. Names the rule that low-margin SKUs lose disproportionate margin to discount and recommends margin-aware discount caps.
assertions: Computes the contribution-margin math step by step
Identifies the 50% margin-retained figure
Names the rule that low-margin SKUs lose disproportionate margin
Recommends margin-aware discount caps by SKU
Warns against applying the same discount across mixed-margin catalog
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Should I run different discount policies on different channels?
evals.expected_output: Affirms yes. Walks channel-by-channel: affiliate and deal sites only convert with codes; direct and email-to-loyal converts at full price. Pulls channel mix data and discount-by-channel intensity. Recommends a channel-specific discount cap policy. Names that discount discipline is channel-specific.
assertions: Recommends channel-specific discount policy
Calls modern.sales.by_channel grouped by discount_band
Names affiliate and deal sites as code-dependent channels
Names direct and loyalist email as full-price channels
Recommends a per-channel discount cap policy
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
